title: Virginie Demont-Breton

infobox.value: Virginie Élodie Marie Thérèse Breton  (1859-07-26)26 July 1859  Courrières, France
(26 July 1859, [Courrières](/wiki/Courri%C3%A8res "Courrières") – 10 January 1935, [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris")) was a French painter.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

French painter

| Virginie Demont-Breton | |
| --- | --- |
| Virginie Demont-Breton c.1900, Photograph by [Pierre Petit](/wiki/Pierre_Petit_(photographer) "Pierre Petit (photographer)") | |
| Born | Virginie Élodie Marie Thérèse Breton  (1859-07-26)26 July 1859  Courrières, France |
| Died | 10 January 1935(1935-01-10) (aged 75)  Paris, France |
| Known for | [Painting](/wiki/Painting "Painting") |
| Spouse | Adrien Demont  ​ (m. 1880)​ |

**Virginie Élodie Marie Thérèse Demont-Breton** (26 July 1859, [Courrières](/wiki/Courri%C3%A8res "Courrières") – 10 January 1935, [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris")) was a French painter.

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Virginie_Demont-Breton&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Virginie was the daughter of Jules Breton (1827–1906) and the niece of Émile Breton , both renowned painters. Her mother, Élodie de Vigne, a painter and model, was herself the daughter of the Flemish painter and medievalist Félix de Vigne . Through her father she was introduced to other painters, the most influential being [Rosa Bonheur](/wiki/Rosa_Bonheur "Rosa Bonheur") who became a role-model and mentor to Virginie.[[1]](#cite_note-1) She married the painter [Adrien Demont](/wiki/Adrien_Demont "Adrien Demont") in 1880.

Her artistic career got off to an early start due to her having family ties with painters, and she finished her first painting at the young age of fourteen.[[2]](#cite_note-2) By the age of twenty, she was exhibiting at the [Salon](/wiki/Salon_(Paris) "Salon (Paris)") where she received an Honorable Mentions and, four years later, she won a gold medal at the [Amsterdam Exposition](/wiki/Internationale_Koloniale_en_Uitvoerhandel_Tentoonstelling "Internationale Koloniale en Uitvoerhandel Tentoonstelling").

In 1890, she and her husband moved to [Wissant](/wiki/Wissant "Wissant"), a small village on the [Côte d'Opale](/wiki/C%C3%B4te_d%27Opale "Côte d'Opale"), where they built a villa designed by the Belgian architect [Edmond De Vigne](/w/index.php?title=Edmond_De_Vigne&action=edit&redlink=1 "Edmond De Vigne (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edmond_De_Vigne "fr:Edmond De Vigne")]. Called the "Typhonium", it is in [Neo-Egyptian](/wiki/Egyptian_Revival_architecture "Egyptian Revival architecture") style and has been a [Historical Monument](/wiki/Monument_historique "Monument historique") since 1985.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Demont-Breton exhibited her work at the [Palace of Fine Arts](/wiki/Museum_of_Science_and_Industry_(Chicago) "Museum of Science and Industry (Chicago)"), the Children's Building, and [The Woman's Building](/wiki/The_Woman%27s_Building_(Chicago) "The Woman's Building (Chicago)") at the 1893 [World's Columbian Exposition](/wiki/World%27s_Columbian_Exposition "World's Columbian Exposition") in Chicago, Illinois.[[4]](#cite_note-Nichols-4)

She served as President of the *[Union of Women Painters and Sculptors](/wiki/Union_of_Women_Painters_and_Sculptors "Union of Women Painters and Sculptors")* from 1895 to 1901, though she resigned for a short period of time in 1892, due to a disagreement between her and the Union's community over what she saw as their unfair methods of voting.[[5]](#cite_note-5) She worked with [Hélène Bertaux](/wiki/H%C3%A9l%C3%A8ne_Bertaux "Hélène Bertaux") in her effort to open the [École des Beaux-Arts](/wiki/%C3%89cole_des_Beaux-Arts "École des Beaux-Arts") to women students; a goal which was achieved in 1897. Thanks to her success in this endeavor, female artists were given the opportunity to not only be present in academic settings, but also the ability to use artistic tools previously not available to them, such as nude models.[[6]](#cite_note-6) She was the second woman to be decorated with the [Légion d'honneur](/wiki/L%C3%A9gion_d%27honneur "Légion d'honneur") – the first woman being her mentor Rosa Bonheur – in 1894,[[7]](#cite_note-7) and became an Officer in 1914. The previous year, she had been elected to the [Royal Academy of Fine Arts (Antwerp)](/wiki/Royal_Academy_of_Fine_Arts_(Antwerp) "Royal Academy of Fine Arts (Antwerp)") in 1913

Originally, she painted portraits and historical scenes but, after moving to Wissant, switched to painting the fishermen and their families in a [Realistic](/wiki/Realism_(art) "Realism (art)") style. In 1889, [Vincent van Gogh](/wiki/Vincent_van_Gogh "Vincent van Gogh") painted his own version of one of her works, *[L’Homme Est en Mer](/wiki/Copies_by_Vincent_van_Gogh#Copy_after_Virginie_Demont_Breton "Copies by Vincent van Gogh")* (*Her Man is Out to Sea*). She also painted scenes of motherhood and children that depict mothers in strong and powerful imagery within nature.

## Writings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Virginie_Demont-Breton&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Writings")]

* *Tendresses dans la tourmente. 1914-1919 poésies*, Alphonse Lemerre, Paris 1920
* *Les maisons que j'ai connues*. Plon-Nourrit, Paris 1926
